did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper presents a deep learning model (specifically a deep neural network pretrained on ImageNet combined with a logistic regression model) trained on digital biopsy frozen section whole slide images to automate the detection of basal cell carcinoma (BCC). The study achieved a sensitivity of 0.73, specificity of 0.88, and an AUC of 0.9 on the test set.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The provided text contains two distinct original research studies. The first study (Kim et al.) presents a deep learning model for automated detection of basal cell carcinoma, detailing methods (training on 200 WSIs), results (sensitivity 0.73, specificity 0.88, AUC 0.9), and conclusions. The second study (Ottwell et al.) is a cross-sectional analysis of randomized clinical trials to assess the effects of lowering P-value thresholds, reporting specific statistical findings (67.2% of endpoints remained significant). Both are original experimental/observational studies, not reviews.

title: Artificial Intelligence Automates Detection of Basal Cell Carcinoma in Frozen Section Biopsies
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning model to automate the detection of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) in digital biopsy frozen section whole slide images. The algorithm, comprising a deep neural network pretrained on ImageNet and a logistic regression model, was trained and tested on 200 whole slide images from 1,769 patient encounters at Mayo Clinic Rochester. Annotations for structures of interest were provided by a Mohs fellow, senior dermatopathologist, and senior Mohs surgeon. The model's performance was evaluated on a held-out test set, achieving a sensitivity of 0.73, specificity of 0.88, and an area under the receiver-operator curve of 0.9. The findings suggest that AI tools can feasibly detect BCC in frozen sections despite variations in tissue quality, potentially enhancing preoperative clinical decision support in Mohs micrographic surgery.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for automated detection using a deep learning model trained on 200 digital biopsy frozen section whole slide images. 88 out of 200 WSIs contained BCC.

all_task_types.target: Presence of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) in frozen section biopsies
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task to predict the presence of BCC. The model uses a deep neural network (DNN) pretrained on ImageNet for patch-level predictions, which are then used to fit a logistic regression (LR) model to make whole slide image (WSI)-level predictions. Performance metrics include sensitivity (0.73), specificity (0.88), and AUC (0.9).

datasets_used.name: Mayo Clinic Rochester Frozen Section Biopsy Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of digital biopsy frozen section whole slide images (WSIs) selected at random from patient encounters at Mayo Clinic Rochester between January 2019 and March 2020. The dataset includes annotations for structures of interest (BCC, actinic keratosis, hair follicle, squamous cell carcinoma) provided by a Mohs fellow, senior dermatopathologist, and senior Mohs surgeon.

use.training: Used to train a deep neural network (DNN) pretrained on ImageNet and a logistic regression (LR) model. The 70% split (140 WSIs) was used for training.
use.validation: Used for tuning the model. The 15% split (30 WSIs) was used for tuning.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance (sensitivity, specificity, AUC). The 15% split (30 WSIs) was used as the held-out test set.

datasets_used.name: Medical Student Pre-clinical Lecture Image Bank
datasets_used.description: A compiled bank of all clinical images from pre-clinical lectures representing a clinical diagnosis from 2017-2019 at an unspecified institution (implied by the author affiliations to be associated with the second study in the abstract collection, likely St. Joseph Mercy or similar, but the text describes it as an 'institutional analysis'). Images were correlated with approximate Fitzpatrick skin types.

use.other: Used for a cross-sectional analysis to assess the representation of skin of color (SoC) in medical education materials.

datasets_used.name: Randomized Clinical Trials (RCT) Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of primary endpoints from randomized clinical trials published in the top 5 dermatology journals between January 1, 2020, and December 31, 2021.

use.other: Used for a cross-sectional analysis to evaluate the effects of lowering the P-value threshold from .05 to .01 on the statistical significance of primary endpoints.

datasets_used.name: Patch Test Patient Cohort
datasets_used.description: A prospective series of 247 consecutive patients with a clinical diagnosis of eczema who were patch-tested with the Spanish standard patch test series and additional allergens (limonene and linalool hydroperoxides).

use.other: Used to assess the clinical relevance of patch test positivity to limonene and linalool hydroperoxides.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To fit WSI-level predictions for the presence of basal cell carcinoma using patch-level predictions from a deep neural network.
To evaluate the performance of the AI model for detecting basal cell carcinoma, specifically calculating the Area Under the Curve (AUC).
To assess the effect of lowering the statistical significance threshold from .05 to .01 on primary endpoints in randomized clinical trials.

hypothesis_testing.test: Comparison of P-value thresholds (specifically evaluating the proportion of endpoints remaining significant when lowering the threshold from < .05 to < .01)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: Lowering the P-value threshold from .05 to .01 does not significantly change the proportion of primary endpoints in randomized clinical trials that remain statistically significant.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: Lowering the P-value threshold from .05 to .01 significantly reduces the proportion of primary endpoints in randomized clinical trials that remain statistically significant.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study found that 67.2% of endpoints (90 of 134) that were significant at P < .05 remained significant at the stricter threshold of P < .01. The remaining 32.8% would not maintain significance. The authors interpret this to mean that lowering the threshold may allow for more reliable results to support medical decisions, though it would reverse conclusions in some cases (e.g., a study on seborrheic dermatitis).

Mayo Clinic Rochester Frozen Section Bio.dataset_description: 200 digital biopsy frozen section whole slide images (WSIs) from 1,769 patient encounters at Mayo Clinic Rochester (Jan 2019 - Mar 2020). 88 WSIs contained Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC). Split into 70-15-15 train-tune-test sets.
Mayo Clinic Rochester Frozen Section Bio.experiment: Automated detection of BCC using Deep Neural Network (DNN) pretrained on ImageNet and Logistic Regression (LR) model
Mayo Clinic Rochester Frozen Section Bio.notes: The model was evaluated on the held-out test set. Findings suggest feasibility of algorithmic tools for BCC detection in frozen sections despite variation in tissue quality.

Medical Student Pre-clinical Lecture Ima.dataset_description: Bank of all clinical images from pre-clinical lectures representing a clinical diagnosis from 2017-2019, correlated with approximate Fitzpatrick skin types.
Medical Student Pre-clinical Lecture Ima.experiment: Assessment of Skin of Color (SoC) representation in medical education materials
Medical Student Pre-clinical Lecture Ima.notes: Findings are consistent with data on SoC representation in major textbooks and board preparatory materials.

Randomized Clinical Trials (RCT) Dataset.dataset_description: Primary endpoints from randomized clinical trials published in the top 5 dermatology journals between January 1, 2020, and December 31, 2021. 106 trials with 166 primary endpoints included.
Randomized Clinical Trials (RCT) Dataset.experiment: Assessment of effects of lowering P-value threshold from < .05 to < .01
Randomized Clinical Trials (RCT) Dataset.notes: Lowering the threshold would reverse conclusions in some cases (e.g., a study on seborrheic dermatitis). No trial characteristics were significantly associated with an RCT reporting at least 1 P value less than .01.

Patch Test Patient Cohort.dataset_description: 247 consecutive patients with clinical diagnosis of eczema patch-tested with Spanish standard series and limonene/linalool hydroperoxides.
Patch Test Patient Cohort.experiment: Assessment of clinical relevance of patch test positivity to limonene and linalool hydroperoxides
Patch Test Patient Cohort.notes: The prevalence of clinically relevant reactions was very low (0.4%). Routine testing for these allergens is not recommended; analyzing possible sources of exposure is advised instead.

architecture_used.main_model: Deep Neural Network (DNN) combined with Logistic Regression (LR)

architecture_details.description: A deep neural network (DNN) was used to generate patch-level predictions from whole slide images (WSIs). These patch-level predictions were then used as input features to fit a logistic regression (LR) model to make WSI-level predictions.
architecture_details.model_family: Deep Neural Network (DNN) and Logistic Regression

training_details.dataset: 200 digital biopsy frozen section whole slide images (WSIs) from Mayo Clinic Rochester

classification_or_output_head.output: WSI-level predictions for the presence of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Deep neural network (DNN) pretrained on ImageNet
preprocessing_techniques: Selection of digital biopsy frozen section whole slide images (WSIs) at random regardless of tissue quality or completeness
Division of whole slide images into 896x896-pixel patches
conclusions: A deep learning model combining a deep neural network (DNN) pretrained on ImageNet and a logistic regression (LR) model can feasibly detect basal cell carcinoma (BCC) in digital biopsy frozen section whole slide images despite variations in tissue quality and tumor type.
The AI model achieved a sensitivity of 0.73, specificity of 0.88, and an area under the receiver-operator curve (AUC) of 0.9 on the held-out test set.

The findings suggest that algorithmic tools for BCC detection may eventually provide preoperative clinical decision support to enhance workflow and patient-provider experience in Mohs micrographic surgery.

Skin of color (Fitzpatrick types IV-VI) is underrepresented in medical student pre-clinical coursework, comprising approximately 17% of images in the MS1 curriculum (2017-2018) and 12% in MS2 dermatology lectures (2019).

Better representation of diverse skin types in medical education is necessary to contribute to comprehensive medical education, improve diagnostic accuracy and cultural competency, and positively impact patient care.

In a cross-sectional analysis of randomized clinical trials in top dermatology journals, 67.2% of primary endpoints that were statistically significant at P < .05 remained significant when the threshold was lowered to P < .01.

Lowering the P-value threshold from .05 to .01 may allow for more reliable results to support medical decisions, although it would reverse conclusions in some cases (e.g., a study on seborrheic dermatitis).
The prevalence of clinically relevant patch test reactions to limonene and linalool hydroperoxides was very low (0.4%) in patients with eczema.
Routine testing for limonene and linalool hydroperoxides is not recommended; instead, analyzing possible sources of exposure for each patient is advised.
important_remarks: The AI model was trained on a small dataset of only 200 whole slide images (WSIs), with 88 containing the target class (BCC), which limits statistical power and generalizability.
The study utilized a single-institution dataset (Mayo Clinic Rochester) without external validation, raising concerns about potential population or scanner-specific bias.
WSIs were selected at random regardless of tissue quality or completeness, introducing significant variability and potential noise into the training data.
The reported sensitivity (0.73) is relatively low for a diagnostic tool intended to detect cancer, indicating a substantial false-negative rate that could impact clinical safety.

The paper is a conference abstract containing multiple distinct studies (AI detection, medical student imagery bias, P-value analysis, patch testing), which may dilute the focus and depth of the primary AI contribution.
No confidence intervals or p-values were reported for the AI model's performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, AUC), limiting the assessment of statistical significance.
The deep learning architecture details are sparse; only 'DNN pretrained on ImageNet' and 'Logistic Regression' are specified, lacking specifics on backbone architecture, layers, or hyperparameters.

The study acknowledges that the AI tool is for 'preoperative clinical decision support' rather than definitive diagnosis, highlighting its current role as an adjunct rather than a replacement for pathologists.
